text: The patient is positioned flat for the laparoscopic ultra low anterior resection. The omentum is moved towards the stomach, and the small bowel is repositioned to fall away from the field of dissection, ensuring the area up to the DJ flexure is clear.
This is a good tip because we always use a gauze whenever there is some small oozing to clean up the field so that the light does not absorb

The surgeon identifies the right ureter and makes a small incision on the peritoneum covering the mesocolon of the sigmoid at the level of the sacral promontory. The incision is extended superficially, allowing carbon dioxide to open the planes. A small plane begins to open up, and the peritoneal incision is carried towards and slightly beyond the root of the inferior mesenteric artery to facilitate the removal of lymphovascular structures.
The surgeon prepares to ligate the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) at its root due to the low level of anastomosis at the pelvic floor, ensuring minimal tension on the left colic artery.

The surgeon uses traction and counter traction to facilitate dissection, with carbon dioxide insufflation visibly opening up the avascular planes. The total mesorectal excision (TME) plane is clearly visible and expanding.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery at low wattage, around 25 to 30 watts, to minimize fumes. The dissection progresses cranially towards the take-off of the inferior mesenteric artery from the aorta.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook or spatula to perform colorectal resections, applying traction and counter traction to facilitate dissection. Carbon dioxide insufflation visibly opens up the tissue planes, guiding the direction of further dissection.
The surgeon extends the incision beyond the root of the inferior mesenteric artery, proceeding towards the lower border of the pancreas to ligate the inferior mesenteric vein at this level.

The surgeon identifies two avascular planes, focusing on the right plane where the fat is elevated along with the specimen. The pulsation of the superior rectal artery is visible, and the dissection is performed close to the vascular sheath.

The surgeon pushes a vessel upwards while working just below the vascular sheath. The ureter on the left side is identified before proceeding with dissection near the inferior mesenteric artery and prior to ligating the artery.

The surgeon identifies the correct dissection plane, known as the 'holy plane' described by Professor Bill Heald, within the total mesorectal excision (TME) approach, ensuring precise anatomical navigation to avoid ureter injury.

The surgeon dissects between the peritoneum of the viscera and the presacral fascia, ensuring the ureter remains distant from the field of dissection. The dissection progresses inferiorly, maintaining a safe distance from the ureter, and follows the identified plane cranially towards the take-off of the inferior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery with a hook to perform precise dissection, carefully coagulating thin tissue layers to progress within the correct anatomical planes.

The surgeon identifies a vessel indicating a deeper dissection layer and adjusts to return to the correct plane. The thin fascia covering the ureter is preserved to prevent exposure of the ureters and gonadal vessels. The surgeon identifies the ureter with its covering fascia, confirming the correct dissection plane.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to take down small vessels and nerve branches running towards the colon, working posterior to the vascular sheath pedicle. The dissection progresses to the root of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), where a vessel sealer is used to seal lymphatic channels, minimizing postoperative lymphorrhea.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes lymphovascular structures from the base of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and over the aorta, ensuring preservation of the hypogastric nerve plexus by avoiding excessive dissection on the inferior aspect. The dissection progresses beyond the level of the IMA takeoff, focusing on removing lymphovascular tissue.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect and seal tissues around the small branches leading to the mesocolon of the descending colon. The vessel is clearly visible, and a right angle or Maryland dissector is used to maneuver around it. The field of dissection is clear of intervening structures, allowing for securement of the vessel with a clip, complemented by either a sealer or cold scissors.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to secure the vessel, followed by applying a clip or ligature. In cases with limited pedicle availability, cold scissors are used for precise dissection. This approach is particularly cautious in elderly patients due to the potential for atherosclerotic vessels, which may bleed post-operatively.

The surgeon identifies the fascia covering the ureter and the gonadal vein, with the peritoneum and the line of Toldt visible in the dissection area. The psoas muscle briefly appears, and the avascular plane is maintained, allowing clear visualization of the bluish tubular gonadal vein.

The surgeon identifies the psoas muscle and proceeds with dissection laterally, marking the medial limit of the dissection. An incision is made on the peritoneum to open the planes, extending towards the lower border of the pancreas. The bluish tubular structure visible is the inferior mesenteric vein, and the dissection is performed near the duodenojejunal flexure, with peritoneal attachments visible. The small bowel is retracted using an assisting hand to maintain a clear surgical field.

The surgeon carefully dissects the thin peritoneal layer, lifting the inferior mesenteric vein to develop the avascular plane inferior to the inferior mesenteric artery. An instrument is used to lift the vein, ensuring the peritoneum covering Gerota's fascia is pushed down, preserving the perinephric fat pad. Blunt suction is used to meticulously separate the thin peritoneum from the mesocolon.

The surgeon follows the CME plane for colon resection, working in an avascular space between the Gerota's fascia and the mesocolon. Blunt traction is used to meticulously push the fascia towards the left shoulder until the spleen is visible, marking the dissection limit. The pancreas is identified and pointed out with an instrument, clearly visible due to the patient's lack of visceral obesity.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the splenic flexure from the medial side using the medial to lateral technique. The inferior mesenteric vein is taken high at the lower border of the pancreas to ensure sufficient colon length for anastomosis at the pelvic floor.

The surgeon isolates and ligates the inferior mesenteric vein at the lower border of the pancreas using a sealer. The mesentery above the pancreas is divided, revealing the anterior surface of the pancreas. The dissection continues along the lower border of the pancreas, with attachments being released to facilitate splenic flexure mobilization.

The surgeon tunnels below the mesentery on top of the pancreas, entering the lesser sac. The stomach is visible, along with the white space of the lesser sac. Vessels in the gastrocolic omentum and the transverse colon are seen. The surgeon incises attachments laterally towards the left side, approaching the spleen. The gonadal vessel appears as a blue structure, with its fascia preserved.

The surgeon uses carbon dioxide to open the surgical plane, increasing the incision on the peritoneum to release the colon. Small flimsy attachments are tackled using blunt and sharp dissection techniques, with a monopolar device aiding in the dissection towards the splenic flexure area from the lateral aspect. The inferior portion and posterior area of the mesocolon have been previously dissected, facilitating the medial to lateral mobilization of the splenic flexure.

The surgeon uses traction and counter-traction to open up the planes, revealing small thin attachments of fascia. These are released, freeing the descending colon due to prior medial and inferior dissection. The medial to lateral dissection technique is employed, with small fibrous tissues being released using sharp and blunt dissection, without sophisticated energy devices.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to rapidly release adhesions near the splenic flexure. The avascular dissection planes are clearly identified, allowing for smooth progression without encountering major structures. The area previously dissected from the medial aspect is visible, marked by a slight blood tinge.

The surgeon releases a small fibrous strand, revealing a gauze placed on the medial side as a marker. Thin attachments are dissected to enhance colon mobility from the splenic flexure area. The spleen becomes visible, and the dissection progresses rapidly with the use of electrocautery, freeing the area over the kidney and near the spleen.

The surgeon releases omental attachments near the splenic flexure and distal transverse mesocolon to ensure tension-free mobilization of the bowel. The lateral attachments are visibly released, and further dissection of the gastrocolic omentum is performed to free the mesocolon. Monopolar and bipolar energy sources are used to manage small mesenteric vessels that may bleed during the procedure.

The surgeon extends the lateral incision cranially and caudally, progressing the dissection in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane. The dissection is performed just below the sacral promontory, staying close to the mesocolon and mesorectum to avoid entering the presacral fascia. The use of a vessel sealer is noted for managing the middle rectal artery, which is larger in this case.

The surgeon works close to the mesorectum, revealing the left hypogastric nerve, which is clearly visible and preserved. The ureter is also visible, covered by fascia, demonstrating precise dissection in the correct anatomical planes.

The surgeon performs dissection on the lateral part, leaving the peritoneum intact, which is then incised to mobilize the descending colon. A combination of sharp and blunt dissection is used, with short bursts of cautery applied. The nerves are visible just below the dissection area, covered by a thin fascial plane. The surgeon carefully pushes the nerve away from the field of dissection while using cautery, ensuring nerve preservation.

The surgeon applies traction and counter-traction to enhance visual appreciation of the anatomy, working close to the pelvic sidewall. Care is taken to identify the correct dissection plane to avoid injury to the internal iliac vein and ureter, which are in close proximity. The use of carbon dioxide insufflation aids in opening up the dissection planes, allowing for precise surgical navigation.

The surgeon applies traction and counter traction to expose the dissection planes clearly, ensuring the dissection does not go too deep. The anatomical structures are carefully manipulated to identify and maintain the correct surgical plane.

The surgeon maintains close proximity to the mesorectum, avoiding excessive inferior dissection. As traction is released, the fascia is observed to fall and fuse with the pelvic sidewall or sacral hollow. The planes become well-defined, allowing for further progression. The surgeon incises slightly adherent tissues at a precise level, guided by previous work on the anterior, anterolateral, and posterior aspects.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the thin fascia, maintaining its integrity, while performing a posterior dissection before moving to the lateral aspects. The clear visualization of the surgical field aids in identifying and preserving critical structures, despite the technical challenges posed by the narrow male pelvis and bulky mesentery.

The surgeon continues the total mesorectal excision (TME), with clear visualization of the planes and minimal presacral fat. Prominent vessels are visible, including the mesorectum and a large vessel in the midline, likely the median sacral vessel. The nerve is visible to the left of the dissection area, and carbon dioxide insufflation aids in opening the planes. Fumes from cautery temporarily obscure vision near the pelvic floor.

The surgeon continues dissection along the pelvic side wall and levator, moving towards the lateral aspect. The posterior dissection is completed, allowing focus on the lateral and anterior aspects. Precise dissection is evident, with muscle contraction visible during cautery use, indicating proximity to the pelvic floor. Traction and counter traction are applied, facilitating the opening of tissue planes, guided by carbon dioxide insufflation.

The surgeon stretches the tissues with traction and counter traction to clearly identify small nerve branches and a thick vessel, identified as the middle rectal artery, supplying the middle of the rectum. Dissection progresses towards the pelvic floor, moving anteriorly without incising the peritoneum, using a vessel sealer in the middle rectal area.

The surgeon uses traction and counter-traction to guide dissection near the right seminal vesicle, preserving a small fascia and fat layer for nerve protection. A monopolar cautery is used to coagulate small vessels. The peritoneum is incised to enter the correct plane, working just below the prostate, which will soon be visible.

The surgeon is dissecting near the prostate, aiming to take the Denonvilliers fascia along with the specimen for additional anterior margin. The prostate is visible close to the surgical instrument. The dissection proceeds between the layers of the Denonvilliers fascia, leaving the prostate bare. On the left side, the pelvic sidewall and levator muscles are visible, with dissection occurring superior to them using cautery. A small portion of the peritoneum is fused in the midline and is being incised with monopolar cautery.

The surgeon uses a small burst of cautery to release the peritoneum, revealing the prostate more clearly. Dissection continues on the left side, with traction and counter-traction aiding in identifying the difference between mesorectal fat and fat surrounding the seminal vesicle. The surgeon progresses through an avascular plane with minimal bleeding, aiming to incise the Denonvilliers' fascia and maintain the correct dissection plane.

The surgeon works close to the left seminal vesicle, using short bursts of cautery and a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques. The pelvic side wall is clearly visible, with twitching of the levator muscles observed as the dissection progresses towards the lateral portion. Facial strands between the pelvic side wall and rectum are clearly dissected, and further anterior dissection is performed to release attachments, aiming for a cylindrical dissection. The planes open up nicely as the dissection continues.

The surgeon continues dissection on the left side, revealing the shape of the prostate located superiorly in the surgical field. Short bursts of cautery are used to join the dissection planes from the left and right sides, progressing further in the correct anatomical plane.

The surgeon continues the dissection around the colorectal area, emphasizing the importance of traction and counter-traction to maintain clear visibility of the thin fascial planes. The remaining attachments on the lateral aspect are being addressed, with a small vessel branch visible near the pelvic side wall, which will be managed shortly.

The surgeon coagulates a small bleeder on the prostate using cautery, applying counter traction to better identify the dissection planes. The dissection progresses towards the inferior portion of the bladder, revealing the denonvillia fascia clearly, which is being taken with the specimen.

The surgeon dissects above the denonvillia fascia, which appears as a taut structure on the rectal surface. Small vessels on the prostate are visible, and the tissue is left with the prostate to avoid leaving it bare. The dissection aims to maintain a good circumferential resection margin (CRM) by taking an additional margin anteriorly.

The surgeon carefully dissects the lateral portion near the Denonvilliers fascia, applying gentle traction and cautery to manage small venules and prevent bleeding. Pulsations from the middle rectal vessel branches are visible, which are being sealed to control blood supply to the rectum. Inflamed tissues and lymphatic fluid are present, requiring gauze placement for fluid management.

The surgeon performs further dissection on the right side, encountering edematous tissues that generate smoke during cautery use. The surgical field is maintained clean by ensuring the hook tip is free of tissue debris, allowing for precise dissection despite the challenging anatomy.

very good results for the patient which would be totally worth the procedure and as well as the maximum benefit can be offered to the patient. So this will improve the quality of the life for the patient and also the quality of the urogenital functions for the patient in the post-operative period. So, we do not have to be worried about the urinary retention or sexual impotence and other problems arising out of these. But we have to always remember that it is not only the surgery which can lead to all this, even radiation and the chemotherapy which we have can really cause some amount of damage to these nerves. So, the results will never be as good as in a normal patient.

The surgeon meticulously manages small oozes and blood vessels to maintain a clear surgical field. The pelvic side wall on the right side becomes visible, similar to the left side, as the dissection continues to further isolate the rectum.

The surgeon carefully works close to the prostate, visible just above the hook, while pushing down the thick, white Denonvilliers fascia. As the dissection progresses caudally, the fascia is incised at a point to transition to the normal plane, avoiding nerve and urethral injury.

The surgeon incises the denonvillia fascia sharply using monopolar energy, moving back to the mesorectal plane just below the fascia. Small thin fibrous structures are visible, and hemostasis is maintained effectively in this deeper plane. The denonvillia fascia appears as a clear, white taut tissue.

The surgeon dissects further on the left side, beginning to incise the Denonvilliers fascia at this level, resulting in some expected oozing. The incision reveals the correct surgical plane, with the thick, taut Denonvilliers fascia visible above and free fat below. The assisting hand uses suction to manage the fumes generated in the narrow surgical area.

The surgeon intermittently uses suction to clear the field while incising the Denonvilliers' fascia on the anterior aspect, extending the incision laterally. This creates a clear plane for further dissection, with small venules and vessels visible but not concerning for significant bleeding.

The surgeon uses cautery to sharply incise the edge of the denonvilliers fascia, progressing laterally. The incised edge and the continuation of the fascia on the lateral aspect are clearly visible. Suction is used to remove fumes generated during cautery, improving visibility. The tip of the hook shows some charred tissue, but the dissection continues smoothly in the visible thin plane.

The surgeon completes the incision of the Denonvilliers fascia, carefully dividing the last portion to avoid nerve injuries near the seminal vesicles. The fascia is fully incised, revealing the surrounding fat of the mesorectum, allowing for rapid progression in developing the surgical plane without concern for small vessels.

A lot of people nowadays claim that only with robotic you get a very good precision and we will be able to do all these complicated tasks in a meticulous manner, but I would tend to disagree. The robotic, yes, does have its own advantages. It gives you a very nice stable platform for the surgeon and it gives you a lot of flexibility. It is less turn-averse on the surgeon to perform the surgery. but nevertheless you can offer the same in laparoscopy as well even though it is a little more cumbersome for the operating surgeon. Right now the operative costs with the robotics are quite expensive and I don't think it's suitable for the Indian scenario yet for most of the patients. for some patients who have a pretty good insurance coverage and they don't really mind about spending it and then for them it does make sense to do it robotically because it's way less turnovers for the surgeon but even patients who are not really able to afford robotic surgery can really

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to dissect the last adherent portion of the Denonvilliers fascia around the rectum, progressing towards the pelvic floor. The dissection is performed circumferentially at the pelvic floor level.

The surgeon completes the total mesorectal excision (TME) by releasing the remaining strands of the Denonvilliers fascia at the pelvic floor. Traction and counter-traction techniques are used to open up the dissection planes. Short bursts of monopolar cautery are applied to control small oozes, with the option to use bipolar cautery for more effective sealing of venules.

The surgeon identifies the exact location of the Denonvilliers fascia and works below it to achieve a comprehensive dissection. The tip of the instrument is cleaned, reducing the fumes generated during dissection. The tissue is less inflamed, allowing for straightforward dissection in a true avascular plane. The surgeon performs a cylindrical dissection by working all around the structure.

It is in these patients where the pelvis is very narrow and we do not have much of a working space to perform that a trans-anal TME also would come into view or a trans-anal minimal invasive surgery like a thymus can come in to help us in performing the dissection very well because even though there is a low working space when we come from the anal end it is quite easy to really do this dissection but the only flip side is you need some special insufflators for that otherwise it becomes very cumbersome to do a thymus And also you need another person who is capable enough to do a pretty decent job on the abdominal side so that you can start performing both the procedures in a concomitant manner. If you try to do one procedure and come down to the next then that is only going to add up to the operative time and it is going to become more difficult for you to do it.

The surgeon isolates the remaining fat around the rectum at the pelvic floor, preparing the area for division. A green 45mm articulating stapler is used to divide the rectal stem, ensuring the rectum is bare. The specimen is pulled outside, and the inferior mesenteric pedicle is excised while coning the mesentery.

The surgeon incises the colon and creates a purse string suture to secure the anvil. The anvil is placed into the purse string, and the spike is brought through the divided portion of the rectum, positioned beyond the pelvic floor. The surgeon uses a stapler to lock the instruments together, ensuring the orange line disappears, indicating proper alignment. The orientation of the bowel is checked for twisting before closing the stapler to perform the end-to-end anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis and insufflates air with saline into the pelvis to check for any leakage.
